Made with MyST

Wasserstein Gradient Flows

Once W2\Wass_2W2​ is a dynamic metric, one can run gradient descent directly on the space of measures. This chapter derives the formal Wasserstein gradient, explains the JKO minimizing-movement scheme, records the role of geodesic convexity in convergence, and then applies the same calculus to mean-field neural-network training.

Guiding Comparison

Euclidean gradient descent moves points along −∇h-\nabla h−∇h. Wasserstein gradient descent moves measures through a continuity equation whose velocity is −∇δf(α)-\nabla\delta f(\alpha)−∇δf(α). The JKO scheme is the implicit Euler step in this geometry.

from pathlib import Path
import sys

from IPython.display import Image as DisplayImage
from IPython.display import display

here = Path.cwd()
myst_dir = None
for candidate in [here, here.parent, here / "myst", here.parent / "myst", here.parent.parent / "myst"]:
    if (candidate / "ot4ml_web.py").exists():
        myst_dir = candidate.resolve()
        sys.path.insert(0, str(myst_dir))
        break

if myst_dir is None:
    raise RuntimeError("Could not locate myst/ot4ml_web.py")

repo_root = myst_dir.parent
thumbnails = repo_root / "notebooks-figures" / "thumbnails"

def show_book_figure(name, width=760):
    display(DisplayImage(filename=str(thumbnails / f"{name}.png"), width=width))

Minimizing Movements and Wasserstein Gradients¶

This first section explains how a variational implicit-Euler step on measures gives rise, in the small-step limit, to a continuity equation driven by the Wasserstein gradient of the energy.

We consider a function f(α)f(\alpha)f(α) and seek a minimizing evolution (αt)t(\alpha_t)_t(αt​)t​. The minimizing-movement strategy over a metric space builds a discrete-time evolution using an implicit Euler scheme:

αt+τ:=arg min⁡α12τW2(αt,α)2+f(α).\alpha_{t+\tau} := \argmin_\alpha \frac{1}{2\tau}\Wass_2(\alpha_t,\alpha)^2+f(\alpha).αt+τ​:=αargmin​2τ1​W2​(αt​,α)2+f(α).
(1)

Euclidean Gradient Flows¶

If (1) is restricted to finite dimensions with αt=δx(t)\alpha_t=\delta_{x(t)}αt​=δx(t)​ and α=δx\alpha=\delta_xα=δx​, it becomes the implicit Euler scheme

x(t+τ):=arg min⁡x12τ∥x−x(t)∥2+h(x),h(x)=f(δx).x(t+\tau) := \argmin_x \frac{1}{2\tau}\norm{x-x(t)}^2+h(x), \qquad h(x)=f(\delta_x).x(t+τ):=xargmin​2τ1​∥x−x(t)∥2+h(x),h(x)=f(δx​).
(2)

Its solution is formally

x(t+τ)=(Id+τ∇h)−1(x(t)).x(t+\tau)=(\Id+\tau\nabla h)^{-1}(x(t)).x(t+τ)=(Id+τ∇h)−1(x(t)).
(3)

In contrast, explicit Euler uses

x(t+τ)=(Id−τ∇h)(x(t))=x(t)−τ∇h(x(t)).x(t+\tau)=(\Id-\tau\nabla h)(x(t))=x(t)-\tau\nabla h(x(t)).x(t+τ)=(Id−τ∇h)(x(t))=x(t)−τ∇h(x(t)).
(4)

Both schemes converge as τ→0\tau\to0τ→0 to the classical gradient flow

x˙(t)=−∇h(x(t)).\dot x(t)=-\nabla h(x(t)).x˙(t)=−∇h(x(t)).
(5)

Wasserstein Gradient Formula¶

The implicit Euler scheme has the advantage that it does not require hhh or fff to be smooth. For fff, this is crucial when evolutions over measures may have densities, atoms or other singular parts.

As τ→0\tau\to0τ→0, under suitable conditions on fff, (1) defines a continuous evolution t↦αtt\mapsto\alpha_tt↦αt​. As in the dynamic formulation, this evolution can be described by a Lagrangian evolution. We use the following first-variation convention: for any β∈P(Rd)\beta\in\Pp(\RR^d)β∈P(Rd) and the signed zero-mass perturbation ρ=β−α\rho=\beta-\alphaρ=β−α,

f((1−τ)α+τβ)=f(α+τρ)=f(α)+τ∫[δf(α)](x) dρ(x)+o(τ).f((1-\tau)\alpha+\tau\beta) = f(\alpha+\tau\rho) = f(\alpha)+ \tau\int[\delta f(\alpha)](x)\d\rho(x) +o(\tau).f((1−τ)α+τβ)=f(α+τρ)=f(α)+τ∫[δf(α)](x)dρ(x)+o(τ).
(6)

The key infinitesimal object is the vector field that represents this differential in the Wasserstein metric.

Definition: Wasserstein Gradient

Assume that fff admits a smooth first variation δf(α)\delta f(\alpha)δf(α). In the smooth formal calculus on P2(Rd)\Pp_2(\RR^d)P2​(Rd), the Wasserstein gradient of fff at α\alphaα is the gradient vector field

∇ ⁣Wf(α)=∇xδf(α).\Wgrad f(\alpha)=\nabla_x\delta f(\alpha).∇W​f(α)=∇x​δf(α).
(7)

The associated formal gradient flow is the continuity equation

∂αt∂t+div⁡(−∇ ⁣Wf(αt)αt)=0.\frac{\partial\alpha_t}{\partial t} +\operatorname{div}(-\Wgrad f(\alpha_t)\alpha_t)=0.∂t∂αt​​+div(−∇W​f(αt​)αt​)=0.
(8)

The following proposition explains why this vector field is the Riemannian gradient for the L2(α)L^2(\alpha)L2(α) metric on velocities.

Proposition: Formal Wasserstein Gradient

Assume that fff admits a smooth first variation δf(α)\delta f(\alpha)δf(α) and that α\alphaα has a smooth positive density. For infinitesimal perturbations generated by a velocity field vvv through (Id+τv)♯α(\Id+\tau v)_\sharp\alpha(Id+τv)♯​α, the differential of fff is

 d dτ∣τ=0f((Id+τv)♯α)=∫⟨∇δf(α)(x),v(x)⟩ dα(x).\frac{\d}{\d\tau}_{|\tau=0} f\big((\Id+\tau v)_\sharp\alpha\big) = \int \dotp{\nabla\delta f(\alpha)(x)}{v(x)}\d\alpha(x).dτd​∣τ=0​f((Id+τv)♯​α)=∫⟨∇δf(α)(x),v(x)⟩dα(x).
(9)

Hence, for the Riemannian metric ∥v∥L2(α)2=∫∥v∥2 dα\norm{v}_{L^2(\alpha)}^2=\int\norm{v}^2\d\alpha∥v∥L2(α)2​=∫∥v∥2dα, the Wasserstein gradient is the vector field

∇ ⁣Wf(α)=∇δf(α).\Wgrad f(\alpha)=\nabla\delta f(\alpha).∇W​f(α)=∇δf(α).
(10)
Proof

The push-forward expansion gives, in the sense of distributions,

(Id+τv)♯α=α−τdiv⁡(αv)+o(τ).(\Id+\tau v)_\sharp\alpha = \alpha-\tau\operatorname{div}(\alpha v)+o(\tau).(Id+τv)♯​α=α−τdiv(αv)+o(τ).
(11)

Using the definition of the first variation,

f((Id+τv)♯α)=f(α)−τ∫δf(α)div⁡(αv) dx+o(τ).f((\Id+\tau v)_\sharp\alpha) = f(\alpha) -\tau\int\delta f(\alpha)\operatorname{div}(\alpha v)\d x +o(\tau).f((Id+τv)♯​α)=f(α)−τ∫δf(α)div(αv)dx+o(τ).
(12)

An integration by parts, with compact support or vanishing boundary flux, gives

−∫δf(α)div⁡(αv) dx=∫⟨∇δf(α),v⟩ dα.-\int\delta f(\alpha)\operatorname{div}(\alpha v)\d x = \int\dotp{\nabla\delta f(\alpha)}{v}\d\alpha.−∫δf(α)div(αv)dx=∫⟨∇δf(α),v⟩dα.
(13)

By definition of the Riesz representative for the L2(α)L^2(\alpha)L2(α) metric, this representative is ∇δf(α)\nabla\delta f(\alpha)∇δf(α).

The Wasserstein gradient-flow viewpoint already appears in John D. Lafferty’s PhD work, published as “The Density Manifold and Configuration Space Quantization”, under the name “density manifold”. It was then systematically developed by Otto, who exposed the formal Riemannian structure of this space Otto, 2001. Rigorous metric-space treatments and numerical JKO schemes can be found in Ambrosio et al., 2006Benamou et al., 2016Peyré, 2015Gallouët & Monsaingeon, 2017.

From the JKO Step to the Velocity Field¶

A first-order expansion of the JKO step explains why (8) uses the vector field ∇ ⁣Wf(α)\Wgrad f(\alpha)∇W​f(α). Write (1) as a minimization over displacement fields vvv such that α=(Id+τv)♯αt\alpha=(\Id+\tau v)_\sharp\alpha_tα=(Id+τv)♯​αt​:

min⁡v12ττ2∥v∥L2(αt)2+f((Id+τv)♯αt).\min_v \frac{1}{2\tau}\tau^2\norm{v}_{L^2(\alpha_t)}^2 + f((\Id+\tau v)_\sharp\alpha_t).vmin​2τ1​τ2∥v∥L2(αt​)2​+f((Id+τv)♯​αt​).
(14)

The push-forward and energy expansions are

(Id+τv)♯αt=αt−τdiv⁡(vαt)+o(τ),(\Id+\tau v)_\sharp\alpha_t = \alpha_t-\tau\operatorname{div}(v\alpha_t)+o(\tau),(Id+τv)♯​αt​=αt​−τdiv(vαt​)+o(τ),
(15)
f((Id+τv)♯αt)=f(αt)−τ∫δf(αt)div⁡(vαt) dx+o(τ)f((\Id+\tau v)_\sharp\alpha_t) = f(\alpha_t) -\tau\int\delta f(\alpha_t)\operatorname{div}(v\alpha_t)\d x +o(\tau)f((Id+τv)♯​αt​)=f(αt​)−τ∫δf(αt​)div(vαt​)dx+o(τ)
(16)

and hence

f((Id+τv)♯αt)=f(αt)+τ∫⟨∇xδf(αt)(x),v(x)⟩ dαt(x)+o(τ).f((\Id+\tau v)_\sharp\alpha_t) = f(\alpha_t) + \tau\int \dotp{\nabla_x\delta f(\alpha_t)(x)}{v(x)} \d\alpha_t(x) +o(\tau).f((Id+τv)♯​αt​)=f(αt​)+τ∫⟨∇x​δf(αt​)(x),v(x)⟩dαt​(x)+o(τ).
(17)

Thus the problem minimized in (1) has the first-order expansion

min⁡vf(αt)+τ∫[12∥v(x)∥2+⟨∇ ⁣Wf(αt)(x),v(x)⟩] dαt(x)+o(τ).\min_v f(\alpha_t) + \tau\int \left[ \frac12\norm{v(x)}^2 + \dotp{\Wgrad f(\alpha_t)(x)}{v(x)} \right] \d\alpha_t(x) +o(\tau).vmin​f(αt​)+τ∫[21​∥v(x)∥2+⟨∇W​f(αt​)(x),v(x)⟩]dαt​(x)+o(τ).
(18)

The pointwise minimizer is v=−∇ ⁣Wf(αt)v=-\Wgrad f(\alpha_t)v=−∇W​f(αt​), which gives the velocity in the continuity equation. We now detail examples of such Wasserstein gradient flows.

show_book_figure("gradflow-jko-entropy-steps")

JKO minimizing movements for the entropy flow in one dimension. The left panel displays successive implicit-Euler minimizers for the heat equation, colored from red to blue. The right panel tracks inverse CDF values Qt(s)=Ft−1(s)Q_t(s)=F_t^{-1}(s)Qt​(s)=Ft−1​(s) for selected probability levels sss, giving a Lagrangian view of the proximal movement in Wasserstein space.

The interactive demo uses the heat-flow representative of the entropy JKO scheme: changing the step size changes the spacing between implicit Euler iterates, while the quantile panel shows how the same movement is seen in Lagrangian coordinates.

Discrete Evolutions¶

If f(α)f(\alpha)f(α) can be evaluated on discrete distributions and ∇ ⁣W\Wgrad∇W​ is continuous in this case, the flow (8) maintains the number of Dirac masses:

αt=1n∑iδxi(t).\alpha_t=\frac1n\sum_i\delta_{x_i(t)}.αt​=n1​i∑​δxi​(t)​.
(19)

The particles X(t)=(xi(t))iX(t)=(x_i(t))_iX(t)=(xi​(t))i​ evolve according to the coupled ODE

x˙i(t)=−n∇xiF(X(t)),\dot x_i(t)=-n\nabla_{x_i}F(X(t)),x˙i​(t)=−n∇xi​​F(X(t)),
(20)

where F(X)=f(1n∑iδxi)F(X)=f\left(\frac1n\sum_i\delta_{x_i}\right)F(X)=f(n1​∑i​δxi​​). The factor nnn comes from the empirical Wasserstein metric 1n∑i∥x˙i∥2\frac1n\sum_i\norm{\dot x_i}^2n1​∑i​∥x˙i​∥2.

Linear Functionals¶

The simplest example is a linear functional

f(α)=∫h(x) dα(x).f(\alpha)=\int h(x)\d\alpha(x).f(α)=∫h(x)dα(x).
(21)

Here δf(α)=h\delta f(\alpha)=hδf(α)=h is independent of α\alphaα. The flow (8) becomes

∂αt∂t+div⁡(−∇h αt)=0.\frac{\partial\alpha_t}{\partial t} + \operatorname{div}(-\nabla h\,\alpha_t)=0.∂t∂αt​​+div(−∇hαt​)=0.
(22)

Thus particles move independently according to the usual gradient flow (5).

Shannon Neg-Entropy¶

A very different behavior is obtained by considering functionals that require αt\alpha_tαt​ to have a density. The canonical example is Shannon neg-entropy

f(α)=∫log⁡( dα dx(x)) dα(x).f(\alpha) = \int \log\left(\frac{\d\alpha}{\d x}(x)\right) \d\alpha(x).f(α)=∫log(dxdα​(x))dα(x).
(23)

Here δf(α)=log⁡( dα dx)\delta f(\alpha)=\log(\frac{\d\alpha}{\d x})δf(α)=log(dxdα​) up to an additive constant, so ∇ ⁣Wf(α)=∇α/α\Wgrad f(\alpha)=\nabla\alpha/\alpha∇W​f(α)=∇α/α, often called the score. The flow (8) becomes the heat equation

∂tαt=Δαt.\partial_t\alpha_t=\Delta\alpha_t.∂t​αt​=Δαt​.
(24)

Other entropy functionals lead to nonlinear diffusion equations; finite-volume and particle discretizations are discussed in Carrillo et al., 2015Gianazza et al., 2009Maas, 2011Erbar, 2010.

For example, a generalized entropy

f(α)=∫g( dα dx) dxf(\alpha)=\int g\left(\frac{\d\alpha}{\d x}\right)\d xf(α)=∫g(dxdα​)dx
(25)

for a scalar convex function ggg leads, in the smooth-density regime, to

∂αt∂t=Δ(P(αt)),\frac{\partial\alpha_t}{\partial t} = \Delta(P(\alpha_t)),∂t∂αt​​=Δ(P(αt​)),
(26)

where the pressure PPP satisfies P′(s)=sg′′(s)P'(s)=s g''(s)P′(s)=sg′′(s). For g(s)=slog⁡sg(s)=s\log sg(s)=slogs, one has P(s)=sP(s)=sP(s)=s and recovers (23); for g(s)=sm/(m−1)g(s)=s^m/(m-1)g(s)=sm/(m−1) with m>1m>1m>1, one obtains P(s)=smP(s)=s^mP(s)=sm up to an additive constant and the porous-medium equation.

A celebrated theorem by McCann McCann, 1997 states that an internal energy of the form (25), for g:R+→R∪{+∞}g:\RR^+\to\RR\cup\{+\infty\}g:R+→R∪{+∞} with g(0)=0g(0)=0g(0)=0, is geodesically convex on P(Rd)\Pp(\RR^d)P(Rd) when ggg is convex and the map r↦rdg(r−d)r\mapsto r^d g(r^{-d})r↦rdg(r−d) is convex and nonincreasing on (0,+∞)(0,+\infty)(0,+∞). Examples include g(s)=sqg(s)=s^qg(s)=sq for q>1q>1q>1 and Shannon entropy g(s)=slog⁡sg(s)=s\log sg(s)=slogs. By contrast, g(s)=−log⁡sg(s)=-\log sg(s)=−logs, associated with the reverse KL divergence, does not satisfy this displacement-convexity criterion.

show_book_figure("gradflow-heat-versus-porous-medium")

Entropy-driven Wasserstein gradient flows from the same compact initial density. The heat flow is generated by Shannon entropy g(ρ)=ρlog⁡ρg(\rho)=\rho\log\rhog(ρ)=ρlogρ and instantly develops Gaussian tails. The porous-medium flows use the power entropy g(ρ)=ρm/(m−1)g(\rho)=\rho^m/(m-1)g(ρ)=ρm/(m−1), hence ∂tρ=Δ(ρm)\partial_t\rho=\Delta(\rho^m)∂t​ρ=Δ(ρm): the middle panel has m=2m=2m=2, while the right panel has the stronger nonlinearity m=6m=6m=6, i.e. ∂tρ=Δ(ρ6)\partial_t\rho=\Delta(\rho^6)∂t​ρ=Δ(ρ6). Larger powers diffuse mainly where the density is high, producing a flatter core and a sharper compact free boundary.

The interactive demo isolates the effect of the entropy exponent. The heat curve keeps Gaussian tails, while increasing mmm keeps a compact front and spreads mass mainly from the high-density core.

Interaction Energies¶

To obtain nonlinear evolutions without requiring the measure to have a density, one can consider

f(α):=∬k(x,y) dα(x) dα(y).f(\alpha) := \iint k(x,y)\d\alpha(x)\d\alpha(y).f(α):=∬k(x,y)dα(x)dα(y).
(27)

For a symmetric kernel kkk,

δf(α)(x)=2∫k(x,y) dα(y),∇ ⁣Wf(α)(x)=2∫∇xk(x,y) dα(y).\delta f(\alpha)(x) = 2\int k(x,y)\d\alpha(y), \qquad \Wgrad f(\alpha)(x) = 2\int\nabla_x k(x,y)\d\alpha(y).δf(α)(x)=2∫k(x,y)dα(y),∇W​f(α)(x)=2∫∇x​k(x,y)dα(y).
(28)

For α0=1n∑iδxi\alpha_0=\frac1n\sum_i\delta_{x_i}α0​=n1​∑i​δxi​​, the flow (8) implies the particle system

x˙i(t)=−2n∑j∇k(xi(t),xj(t)).\dot x_i(t) = -\frac2n\sum_j\nabla k(x_i(t),x_j(t)).x˙i​(t)=−n2​j∑​∇k(xi​(t),xj​(t)).
(29)

If kkk is positive definite, or more generally conditionally positive definite on signed measures of zero total mass as for the energy-distance kernel k(x,y)=−∥x−y∥k(x,y)=-\norm{x-y}k(x,y)=−∥x−y∥, and one minimizes the squared kernel discrepancy to a teacher distribution β\betaβ, then

∥α−β∥k2=∬k dα dα−2∫(∫k(x,y) dβ(y)) dα(x)+constant.\norm{\alpha-\beta}_k^2 = \iint k\d\alpha\d\alpha -2\int\left(\int k(x,y)\d\beta(y)\right)\d\alpha(x) +\mathrm{constant}.∥α−β∥k2​=∬kdαdα−2∫(∫k(x,y)dβ(y))dα(x)+constant.
(30)

Thus MMD-type training energies are exactly an interaction energy plus a linear potential. The teacher distribution appears through the potential x↦−2∫k(x,y) dβ(y)x\mapsto-2\int k(x,y)\d\beta(y)x↦−2∫k(x,y)dβ(y), and the corresponding empirical Wasserstein gradient flow is

x˙i(t)=−2n∑j∇xk(xi(t),xj(t))+2∫∇xk(xi(t),y) dβ(y).\dot x_i(t) = -\frac2n\sum_j\nabla_x k(x_i(t),x_j(t)) + 2\int\nabla_x k(x_i(t),y)\d\beta(y).x˙i​(t)=−n2​j∑​∇x​k(xi​(t),xj​(t))+2∫∇x​k(xi​(t),y)dβ(y).
(31)

The first term is a kernelized self-interaction; the second is the attraction induced by the continuous teacher kernel mean. At the continuum level, characteristic positive-definite kernels, and the Euclidean energy-distance kernel on probability measures, have β\betaβ as the unique minimizer of ∥α−β∥k2\norm{\alpha-\beta}_k^2∥α−β∥k2​. For finitely many particles, however, the flow can only form a kernelized quadrature of β\betaβ, and small particle systems may cover the target modes poorly. The particle-count figure below illustrates this finite-particle effect.

show_book_figure("gradflow-mmd-particle-count")

Particle count in the deterministic Wasserstein gradient flow of the squared MMD-type discrepancy to a smooth two-Gaussian teacher distribution, using here the energy-distance kernel k(x,y)=−∥x−y∥k(x,y)=-\norm{x-y}k(x,y)=−∥x−y∥. The teacher itself is shown only through true density contours, while red dots are a compact shifted Gaussian initialization placed away from the target, red-to-blue curves show a thinned subset of particle trajectories, and blue dots show the stabilized long-time particles. With too few particles, the empirical measure forms a sparse kernelized quadrature and may under-cover the target modes; increasing nnn makes the particle cloud approximate the continuous target geometry more faithfully.

The interactive demo turns this finite-particle effect into a parameter: increasing the number of particles makes the same deterministic force field approximate the teacher geometry more faithfully.

show_book_figure("gradflow-interaction-particles")

Interaction-energy particle flows for three choices of kkk. A positive Gaussian kernel k(x,y)=exp⁡(−∥x−y∥2/(2σ2))k(x,y)=\exp(-\norm{x-y}^2/(2\sigma^2))k(x,y)=exp(−∥x−y∥2/(2σ2)) produces short-range repulsion under Wasserstein descent; changing its sign produces attraction and collapse; adding a quadratic long-range attraction to the repulsive kernel yields a balanced attraction--repulsion dynamics. The curves use arclength-based red-to-blue coloring along a longer integration of the coupled particle ODE (20).

The interactive demo lets the sign and strength of the interaction change without editing the hidden particle solver. This is the quickest way to see how the same formal ODE can repel, collapse, or self-organize.

show_book_figure("gradflow-particle-objective-geometries")

Particle trajectories induced by different discrepancy geometries. The red particles and blue target cloud are the same in all panels. Straight OT displacement produces rays from an optimal matching; an MMD-type witness field gives smoother nonlocal forces; the Sinkhorn-divergence force is an entropic, debiased transport attraction; and the normalized drifting field combines attraction to data with self-repulsion. The figure is qualitative: it compares geometric behavior, not solver performance.

The interactive demo keeps the source and target fixed while switching the discrepancy geometry. The smoothing parameter controls how local or nonlocal the induced force appears.

Stochastic Particles and McKean--Vlasov Limits¶

Deterministic particle flows have stochastic counterparts, where Brownian noise at the particle level becomes an entropy term at the measure level. If the drift does not depend on the empirical measure, each particle evolves independently according to

 dXt=b(Xt) dt+2 σ dBt,\d X_t=b(X_t)\d t+\sqrt2\,\sigma\d B_t,dXt​=b(Xt​)dt+2​σdBt​,
(32)

and the one-particle law αt=ρt dx\alpha_t=\rho_t\d xαt​=ρt​dx satisfies the linear Fokker--Planck equation

∂tρt=−div⁡(bρt)+σ2Δρt.\partial_t\rho_t = -\operatorname{div}(b\rho_t)+\sigma^2\Delta\rho_t.∂t​ρt​=−div(bρt​)+σ2Δρt​.
(33)

For example, if b=−∇Vb=-\nabla Vb=−∇V, this is the W2\Wass_2W2​ gradient flow of the free energy

∫Vρ  dx+σ2∫ρlog⁡ρ  dx.\int V\rho\,\d x+\sigma^2\int\rho\log\rho\,\d x.∫Vρdx+σ2∫ρlogρdx.
(34)

The mean-field case is different: the drift is recomputed from the current empirical distribution of all particles,

 dXin(t)=b(Xin(t),μtn) dt+2 σ dBi(t),μtn=1n∑i=1nδXin(t).\d X_i^n(t) = b(X_i^n(t),\mu_t^n)\d t+\sqrt2\,\sigma\d B_i(t), \qquad \mu_t^n=\frac1n\sum_{i=1}^n\delta_{X_i^n(t)}.dXin​(t)=b(Xin​(t),μtn​)dt+2​σdBi​(t),μtn​=n1​i=1∑n​δXin​(t)​.
(35)

For finite nnn, the empirical law μtn\mu_t^nμtn​ is random. Under suitable Lipschitz, growth and chaotic-initialization assumptions, propagation of chaos states that finitely many particles become asymptotically independent as n→∞n\to\inftyn→∞, all with the same deterministic law ρt dx\rho_t\d xρt​dx. Equivalently, μtn\mu_t^nμtn​ converges in probability to this law. The limiting density solves the nonlinear Fokker--Planck, or McKean--Vlasov, equation

∂tρt=−div⁡(b(x,ρt)ρt)+σ2Δρt.\partial_t\rho_t = -\operatorname{div}\big(b(x,\rho_t)\rho_t\big) + \sigma^2\Delta\rho_t.∂t​ρt​=−div(b(x,ρt​)ρt​)+σ2Δρt​.
(36)

When the interaction drift has variational form

b(x,ρ)=−∇δEδρ(x),b(x,\rho) = -\nabla\frac{\delta\mathcal E}{\delta\rho}(x),b(x,ρ)=−∇δρδE​(x),
(37)

this PDE is the Wasserstein gradient flow of the entropy-regularized energy

E(ρ)+σ2∫ρlog⁡ρ  dx.\mathcal E(\rho)+\sigma^2\int\rho\log\rho\,\d x.E(ρ)+σ2∫ρlogρdx.
(38)
show_book_figure("gradflow-fokker-planck-three-representations")

Three numerical representations of the same entropy-regularized Wasserstein gradient flow of KL⁡(ρ∣β)\KL(\rho|\beta)KL(ρ∣β), where β\betaβ is a two-Gaussian target shifted to the right of an initially isotropic Gaussian density. The first row simulates independent Langevin particles and displays a thinned set of trajectories in the left panel. The second row evolves many deterministic particles with velocity τ(∇log⁡β−∇log⁡ρt)\tau(\nabla\log\beta-\nabla\log\rho_t)τ(∇logβ−∇logρt​), estimating ∇log⁡ρt\nabla\log\rho_t∇logρt​ by a sharper kernel-density score; only representative trajectories and particle subsets are displayed. The third row solves the corresponding Fokker--Planck equation on a grid, starting from the initial density in the left panel. The remaining columns use front-loaded times, so that the onset of the flow and the later deformation toward a bimodal law are both visible.

The interactive demo compares three views of the same entropy-regularized relaxation: stochastic Langevin particles, deterministic score particles, and a smoothed grid density. The noise slider controls the entropy strength.

Geodesic Convexity and Convergence¶

Geodesic convexity is the convexity notion adapted to Wasserstein geometry. It is the condition that turns the formal gradient-flow calculus into a convergence theory.

Geodesics and Convexity¶

A constant-speed W2\Wass_2W2​ geodesic between α0\alpha_0α0​ and α1\alpha_1α1​ is obtained, as in the McCann interpolation, from any optimal coupling π⋆∈Π(α0,α1)\pi^\star\in\Couplings(\alpha_0,\alpha_1)π⋆∈Π(α0​,α1​) by

αt=((1−t)P0+tP1)♯π⋆,t∈[0,1],\alpha_t=((1-t)P_0+tP_1)_\sharp\pi^\star, \qquad t\in[0,1],αt​=((1−t)P0​+tP1​)♯​π⋆,t∈[0,1],
(39)

where P0(x,y)=xP_0(x,y)=xP0​(x,y)=x and P1(x,y)=yP_1(x,y)=yP1​(x,y)=y. If the optimal plan is induced by a Brenier map TTT, this reduces to ((1−t)Id+tT)♯α0((1-t)\Id+tT)_\sharp\alpha_0((1−t)Id+tT)♯​α0​. The coupling formula matters because geodesics exist even when no Monge map exists, for instance when a Dirac mass must split.

Definition: Geodesic Convexity

A functional fff on P2(Rd)\Pp_2(\RR^d)P2​(Rd) is geodesically convex if for every W2\Wass_2W2​ geodesic (αt)t(\alpha_t)_t(αt​)t​,

f(αt)≤(1−t)f(α0)+tf(α1).f(\alpha_t) \leq (1-t)f(\alpha_0)+t f(\alpha_1).f(αt​)≤(1−t)f(α0​)+tf(α1​).
(40)

It is λ\lambdaλ-geodesically convex if the right-hand side is improved by

−λ2t(1−t)W22(α0,α1).-\frac{\lambda}{2}t(1-t)\Wass_2^2(\alpha_0,\alpha_1).−2λ​t(1−t)W22​(α0​,α1​).
(41)
Proposition: Basic Geodesically Convex Energies

The following formal statements hold on P2(Rd)\Pp_2(\RR^d)P2​(Rd).

  1. If hhh is convex, then α↦∫h dα\alpha\mapsto\int h\d\alphaα↦∫hdα is geodesically convex; if hhh is λ\lambdaλ-strongly convex, it is λ\lambdaλ-geodesically convex.

  2. If W(x−y)W(x-y)W(x−y) is convex as a function of the displacement, then α↦12∬W(x−y) dα(x) dα(y)\alpha\mapsto\frac12\iint W(x-y)\d\alpha(x)\d\alpha(y)α↦21​∬W(x−y)dα(x)dα(y) is geodesically convex.

  3. Shannon entropy α↦∫ρlog⁡ρ  dx\alpha\mapsto\int\rho\log\rho\,\d xα↦∫ρlogρdx is geodesically convex.

  4. The relative entropy KL⁡(α∣γ)\KL(\alpha|\gamma)KL(α∣γ) with  dγ=e−V dx/Z\d\gamma=e^{-V}\d x/Zdγ=e−Vdx/Z is λ\lambdaλ-geodesically convex when VVV is λ\lambdaλ-strongly convex.

Proof

Along a Monge geodesic Xt=(1−t)X0+tX1X_t=(1-t)X_0+tX_1Xt​=(1−t)X0​+tX1​, convexity of hhh gives h(Xt)≤(1−t)h(X0)+th(X1)h(X_t)\leq(1-t)h(X_0)+t h(X_1)h(Xt​)≤(1−t)h(X0​)+th(X1​), and strong convexity gives the additional quadratic term; integrating proves the first claim.

The interaction claim follows similarly by applying convexity of WWW to pairwise differences Xt−Xt′=(1−t)(X0−X0′)+t(X1−X1′)X_t-X_t'=(1-t)(X_0-X_0')+t(X_1-X_1')Xt​−Xt′​=(1−t)(X0​−X0′​)+t(X1​−X1′​) and integrating over two independent copies. The entropy claim is McCann’s displacement-convexity theorem; at the density level it follows from the concavity of the Jacobian determinant under the interpolation of optimal maps. Finally,

KL⁡(α∣γ)=∫ρlog⁡ρ  dx+∫V dα+constant,\KL(\alpha|\gamma) = \int\rho\log\rho\,\d x+\int V\d\alpha+\mathrm{constant},KL(α∣γ)=∫ρlogρdx+∫Vdα+constant,
(42)

so it is the sum of displacement-convex entropy and a λ\lambdaλ-geodesically convex linear potential.

Convergence of the Flow¶

In general, analyzing (8) is delicate. The cleanest case is when fff is geodesically convex. This condition is the Wasserstein analogue of convexity in Euclidean gradient descent.

Proposition: Energy Decay for Convex Wasserstein Flows

Assume formally that fff is geodesically convex, admits a smooth first variation, and has a minimizer α⋆\alpha^\starα⋆. Let (αt)t(\alpha_t)_t(αt​)t​ be a smooth solution of the Wasserstein gradient flow

∂tαt+div⁡(αtvt)=0,vt=−∇ ⁣Wf(αt).\partial_t\alpha_t+\operatorname{div}(\alpha_t v_t)=0, \qquad v_t=-\Wgrad f(\alpha_t).∂t​αt​+div(αt​vt​)=0,vt​=−∇W​f(αt​).
(43)

Then

 d dtf(αt)=−∫∥∇ ⁣Wf(αt)(x)∥2 dαt(x)≤0.\frac{\d}{\d t}f(\alpha_t) = -\int\norm{\Wgrad f(\alpha_t)(x)}^2\d\alpha_t(x) \leq0.dtd​f(αt​)=−∫∥∇W​f(αt​)(x)∥2dαt​(x)≤0.
(44)

If TtT_tTt​ is the optimal map from αt\alpha_tαt​ to α⋆\alpha^\starα⋆, then

f(αt)−f(α⋆)≤− d dt12W22(αt,α⋆),f(\alpha_t)-f(\alpha^\star) \leq -\frac{\d}{\d t}\frac12\Wass_2^2(\alpha_t,\alpha^\star),f(αt​)−f(α⋆)≤−dtd​21​W22​(αt​,α⋆),
(45)

and consequently

f(αt)−f(α⋆)≤W22(α0,α⋆)2t.f(\alpha_t)-f(\alpha^\star) \leq \frac{\Wass_2^2(\alpha_0,\alpha^\star)}{2t}.f(αt​)−f(α⋆)≤2tW22​(α0​,α⋆)​.
(46)

If fff is λ\lambdaλ-geodesically convex with λ>0\lambda>0λ>0, then

f(αt)−f(α⋆)≤e−2λt(f(α0)−f(α⋆)).f(\alpha_t)-f(\alpha^\star) \leq e^{-2\lambda t}\bigl(f(\alpha_0)-f(\alpha^\star)\bigr).f(αt​)−f(α⋆)≤e−2λt(f(α0​)−f(α⋆)).
(47)
Proof

The chain rule and the formal Wasserstein-gradient proposition give

 d dtf(αt)=∫⟨∇ ⁣Wf(αt)(x),vt(x)⟩ dαt(x)=−∫∥∇ ⁣Wf(αt)(x)∥2 dαt(x).\frac{\d}{\d t}f(\alpha_t) = \int\dotp{\Wgrad f(\alpha_t)(x)}{v_t(x)}\d\alpha_t(x) = -\int\norm{\Wgrad f(\alpha_t)(x)}^2\d\alpha_t(x).dtd​f(αt​)=∫⟨∇W​f(αt​)(x),vt​(x)⟩dαt​(x)=−∫∥∇W​f(αt​)(x)∥2dαt​(x).
(48)

Geodesic convexity along the geodesic ((1−s)Id+sTt)♯αt((1-s)\Id+sT_t)_\sharp\alpha_t((1−s)Id+sTt​)♯​αt​ gives

f(α⋆)−f(αt)≥∫⟨∇ ⁣Wf(αt)(x),Tt(x)−x⟩ dαt(x).f(\alpha^\star)-f(\alpha_t) \geq \int\dotp{\Wgrad f(\alpha_t)(x)}{T_t(x)-x}\d\alpha_t(x).f(α⋆)−f(αt​)≥∫⟨∇W​f(αt​)(x),Tt​(x)−x⟩dαt​(x).
(49)

Since vt=−∇ ⁣Wf(αt)v_t=-\Wgrad f(\alpha_t)vt​=−∇W​f(αt​),

f(αt)−f(α⋆)≤∫⟨vt(x),Tt(x)−x⟩ dαt(x).f(\alpha_t)-f(\alpha^\star) \leq \int\dotp{v_t(x)}{T_t(x)-x}\d\alpha_t(x).f(αt​)−f(α⋆)≤∫⟨vt​(x),Tt​(x)−x⟩dαt​(x).
(50)

The first-variation formula for the squared Wasserstein distance gives

 d dt12W22(αt,α⋆)=∫⟨x−Tt(x),vt(x)⟩ dαt(x),\frac{\d}{\d t}\frac12\Wass_2^2(\alpha_t,\alpha^\star) = \int\dotp{x-T_t(x)}{v_t(x)}\d\alpha_t(x),dtd​21​W22​(αt​,α⋆)=∫⟨x−Tt​(x),vt​(x)⟩dαt​(x),
(51)

which proves the differential inequality. Integrating it from 0 to ttt and using monotonicity of s↦f(αs)s\mapsto f(\alpha_s)s↦f(αs​) gives

t(f(αt)−f(α⋆))≤∫0t(f(αs)−f(α⋆)) ds≤12W22(α0,α⋆).t\bigl(f(\alpha_t)-f(\alpha^\star)\bigr) \leq \int_0^t \bigl(f(\alpha_s)-f(\alpha^\star)\bigr)\d s \leq \frac12\Wass_2^2(\alpha_0,\alpha^\star).t(f(αt​)−f(α⋆))≤∫0t​(f(αs​)−f(α⋆))ds≤21​W22​(α0​,α⋆).
(52)

If fff is λ\lambdaλ-geodesically convex, the Wasserstein analogue of strong convexity gives the slope inequality

∫∥∇ ⁣Wf(αt)∥2 dαt≥2λ(f(αt)−f(α⋆)).\int\norm{\Wgrad f(\alpha_t)}^2\d\alpha_t \geq 2\lambda\bigl(f(\alpha_t)-f(\alpha^\star)\bigr).∫∥∇W​f(αt​)∥2dαt​≥2λ(f(αt​)−f(α⋆)).
(53)

Combining it with the energy dissipation identity yields

 d dt(f(αt)−f(α⋆))≤−2λ(f(αt)−f(α⋆)),\frac{\d}{\d t}\bigl(f(\alpha_t)-f(\alpha^\star)\bigr) \leq -2\lambda\bigl(f(\alpha_t)-f(\alpha^\star)\bigr),dtd​(f(αt​)−f(α⋆))≤−2λ(f(αt​)−f(α⋆)),
(54)

and Gronwall’s lemma gives the exponential rate.

Proposition: Convex Examples Covered by the Theory

The hypotheses of the energy-decay proposition are satisfied in the following standard cases, at least at the formal smooth level used here.

  1. For f(α)=∫h dαf(\alpha)=\int h\d\alphaf(α)=∫hdα, geodesic convexity holds when hhh is convex. If hhh is λ\lambdaλ-strongly convex, then the flow enjoys the exponential rate.

  2. For f(α)=12∬W(x−y) dα(x) dα(y)f(\alpha)=\frac12\iint W(x-y)\d\alpha(x)\d\alpha(y)f(α)=21​∬W(x−y)dα(x)dα(y), geodesic convexity holds when WWW is convex and even. This covers repulsive or attractive pairwise models whose displacement cost has no non-convex wells.

  3. Shannon entropy and, more generally, McCann displacement-convex internal energies generate diffusion-type Wasserstein gradient flows.

  4. If γ=Z−1e−V dx\gamma=Z^{-1}e^{-V}\d xγ=Z−1e−Vdx and VVV is λ\lambdaλ-strongly convex, then KL⁡(α∣γ)\KL(\alpha|\gamma)KL(α∣γ) is λ\lambdaλ-geodesically convex. Its flow is the Fokker--Planck equation with invariant law γ\gammaγ.

Proof

Let (αt)t(\alpha_t)_t(αt​)t​ be the McCann interpolation between α0\alpha_0α0​ and α1\alpha_1α1​, written with an optimal coupling as Xt=(1−t)X0+tX1X_t=(1-t)X_0+tX_1Xt​=(1−t)X0​+tX1​. For a linear energy, Jensen’s inequality gives

h(Xt)≤(1−t)h(X0)+th(X1),h(X_t)\leq(1-t)h(X_0)+t h(X_1),h(Xt​)≤(1−t)h(X0​)+th(X1​),
(55)

and the strong convexity version gives the additional term −λ2t(1−t)∥X0−X1∥2-\frac{\lambda}{2}t(1-t)\norm{X_0-X_1}^2−2λ​t(1−t)∥X0​−X1​∥2. Integrating over the optimal coupling proves geodesic convexity and λ\lambdaλ-geodesic convexity.

For interaction energies, use two independent copies of the optimal coupling. The pairwise displacement evolves as

Xt−Xt′=(1−t)(X0−X0′)+t(X1−X1′).X_t-X_t' = (1-t)(X_0-X_0')+t(X_1-X_1').Xt​−Xt′​=(1−t)(X0​−X0′​)+t(X1​−X1′​).
(56)

Convexity of WWW gives the convexity inequality after integration over the product coupling. Evenness of WWW ensures that the interaction is symmetric in the two particles and matches the usual factor 1/21/21/2 in (27).

The entropy claim is McCann’s displacement-convexity theorem. For smooth positive densities and Brenier maps, it follows from the change-of-variables formula and the concavity of the determinant along positive matrices; the general statement is obtained by approximation. Finally,

KL⁡(α∣γ)=∫ρlog⁡ρ  dx+∫V dα+log⁡Z,\KL(\alpha|\gamma) = \int\rho\log\rho\,\d x+\int V\d\alpha+\log Z,KL(α∣γ)=∫ρlogρdx+∫Vdα+logZ,
(57)

so it is the sum of the displacement-convex entropy and the λ\lambdaλ-geodesically convex linear potential generated by VVV. The energy-decay proposition then applies to all four cases.

Convexity and Curvature¶

The same language is not restricted to subsets of Rd\RR^dRd. If (X,d,m)(\X,\dist,\mathfrak m)(X,d,m) is a geodesic metric-measure space, W2\Wass_2W2​ geodesics can be defined by transporting each pair of endpoints along metric geodesics, or more intrinsically by dynamical optimal plans on path space. Given a reference measure m\mathfrak mm, the entropy relative to m\mathfrak mm is

Entm(α):={∫Xρlog⁡ρ  dm,if α=ρ m,+∞,otherwise.\mathrm{Ent}_{\mathfrak m}(\alpha) \eqdef \begin{cases} \displaystyle\int_\X\rho\log\rho\,\d\mathfrak m, &\text{if }\alpha=\rho\,\mathfrak m,\\ +\infty, &\text{otherwise.} \end{cases}Entm​(α):=⎩⎨⎧​∫X​ρlogρdm,+∞,​if α=ρm,otherwise.​
(58)

On a smooth Riemannian manifold (M,g)(M,g)(M,g), the Ricci curvature tensor Ricg\mathrm{Ric}_gRicg​ is the trace of the Riemann curvature tensor. The lower bound Ricg≥λg\mathrm{Ric}_g\geq\lambda gRicg​≥λg means that Ricg(v,v)≥λ∣v∣g2\mathrm{Ric}_g(v,v)\geq\lambda |v|_g^2Ricg​(v,v)≥λ∣v∣g2​ for every tangent vector vvv. The fundamental link between curvature and optimal transport is that this tensor lower bound is exactly encoded by geodesic convexity of entropy.

Theorem: Ricci Curvature and Entropy Convexity

Let (M,g)(M,g)(M,g) be a smooth compact connected Riemannian manifold without boundary, and let m=volg\mathfrak m=\mathrm{vol}_gm=volg​. For λ∈R\lambda\in\RRλ∈R, the lower Ricci bound Ricg≥λg\mathrm{Ric}_g\geq\lambda gRicg​≥λg holds if and only if Entm\mathrm{Ent}_{\mathfrak m}Entm​ is λ\lambdaλ-geodesically convex on (P2(M),W2)(\Pp_2(M),\Wass_2)(P2​(M),W2​).

This equivalence was developed in the smooth Riemannian setting by Cordero-Erausquin, McCann and Schmuckenschlaeger and by von Renesse and Sturm Cordero-Erausquin et al., 2001Renesse & Sturm, 2005; it is a central theme of the optimal-transport approach to curvature in Villani’s monograph Villani, 2009. Lott--Villani and Sturm then used the same entropy-convexity principle to define synthetic lower Ricci curvature bounds on metric-measure spaces Lott & Villani, 2009Sturm, 2006Sturm, 2006. Outside this convex, curvature-controlled regime, such as in the mean-field neural-network example below, the flow may still be informative but its convergence analysis requires problem-specific arguments.

Training Two-Layer MLPs as Wasserstein Flows¶

Mean-field limits recast the training of wide neural networks as transport of a distribution of neurons. This section shows how the particle ODE of gradient descent becomes a Wasserstein flow in parameter space.

We use z∈Rdz\in\RR^dz∈Rd for the input data and y∈Rd′y\in\RR^{d'}y∈Rd′ for the label. A neuron is a particle

x=(u,v)∈Rd×Rd′,x=(u,v)\in\RR^d\times\RR^{d'},x=(u,v)∈Rd×Rd′,
(59)

where uuu is the inner weight and vvv is the outer vector weight. For a scalar nonlinearity σ\sigmaσ, define the vector-valued feature

ψ(x,z)=v σ(⟨u,z⟩)∈Rd′.\psi(x,z)=v\,\sigma(\dotp{u}{z})\in\RR^{d'}.ψ(x,z)=vσ(⟨u,z⟩)∈Rd′.
(60)

The width-nnn network and its mean-field version are

GX(z)=1n∑i=1nψ(xi,z),Gα(z)=∫ψ(x,z) dα(x),α=1n∑iδxi.G_X(z)=\frac1n\sum_{i=1}^n\psi(x_i,z), \qquad G_\alpha(z)=\int\psi(x,z)\d\alpha(x), \qquad \alpha=\frac1n\sum_i\delta_{x_i}.GX​(z)=n1​i=1∑n​ψ(xi​,z),Gα​(z)=∫ψ(x,z)dα(x),α=n1​i∑​δxi​​.
(61)

This formulation removes the artificial ordering of neurons and allows α\alphaα to be a continuous distribution of infinitely many neurons.

Let ρ\rhoρ be a probability distribution on data-label pairs (z,y)∈Rd×Rd′(z,y)\in\RR^d\times\RR^{d'}(z,y)∈Rd×Rd′. The population risk is

f(α)=∫ℓ(Gα(z),y) dρ(z,y),f(\alpha)=\int\ell(G_\alpha(z),y)\d\rho(z,y),f(α)=∫ℓ(Gα​(z),y)dρ(z,y),
(62)

and the empirical risk is the special case ρ=ρN:=N−1∑k=1Nδ(zk,yk)\rho=\rho_N\eqdef N^{-1}\sum_{k=1}^N\delta_{(z_k,y_k)}ρ=ρN​:=N−1∑k=1N​δ(zk​,yk​)​. Since α↦Gα\alpha\mapsto G_\alphaα↦Gα​ is linear, fff is convex as a function of α\alphaα whenever ℓ(⋅,y)\ell(\cdot,y)ℓ(⋅,y) is convex. For the empirical neuron law αX=n−1∑iδxi\alpha_X=n^{-1}\sum_i\delta_{x_i}αX​=n−1∑i​δxi​​, the Wasserstein metric induces on particles the rescaled metric n−1∑i∥x˙i∥2n^{-1}\sum_i\norm{\dot x_i}^2n−1∑i​∥x˙i​∥2. The corresponding particle flow is

x˙i=−n∇xiF(X),F(X)=f ⁣(1n∑iδxi).\dot x_i=-n\nabla_{x_i}F(X), \qquad F(X)=f\!\left(\frac1n\sum_i\delta_{x_i}\right).x˙i​=−n∇xi​​F(X),F(X)=f(n1​i∑​δxi​​).
(63)

This is the gradient flow of F(X)=f(αX)F(X)=f(\alpha_X)F(X)=f(αX​) for the Wasserstein particle metric, equivalently Euclidean gradient descent with time scale multiplied by nnn. It gives a particle discretization of (8).

Assume that ℓ\ellℓ is differentiable in its first variable. The first variation is

δf(α)(x)=∫⟨∇1ℓ(Gα(z),y),ψ(x,z)⟩ dρ(z,y),\delta f(\alpha)(x) = \int \dotp{\nabla_1\ell(G_\alpha(z),y)}{\psi(x,z)} \d\rho(z,y),δf(α)(x)=∫⟨∇1​ℓ(Gα​(z),y),ψ(x,z)⟩dρ(z,y),
(64)

and the Wasserstein gradient in parameter space is

∇ ⁣Wf(α)(x)=∇xδf(α)(x)=∫[Dxψ(x,z)]⊤∇1ℓ(Gα(z),y) dρ(z,y).\Wgrad f(\alpha)(x) = \nabla_x\delta f(\alpha)(x) = \int [D_x\psi(x,z)]^\top\nabla_1\ell(G_\alpha(z),y) \d\rho(z,y).∇W​f(α)(x)=∇x​δf(α)(x)=∫[Dx​ψ(x,z)]⊤∇1​ℓ(Gα​(z),y)dρ(z,y).
(65)

For the squared Euclidean loss ℓ(s,y)=12∥s−y∥2\ell(s,y)=\frac12\norm{s-y}^2ℓ(s,y)=21​∥s−y∥2, the energy is the sum of a quadratic interaction and a linear potential:

f(α)=12∬k(x,x′) dα(x) dα(x′)+∫g(x) dα(x)+12∫∥y∥2 dρ(z,y),f(\alpha) = \frac12\iint k(x,x')\d\alpha(x)\d\alpha(x') + \int g(x)\d\alpha(x) + \frac12\int\norm{y}^2\d\rho(z,y),f(α)=21​∬k(x,x′)dα(x)dα(x′)+∫g(x)dα(x)+21​∫∥y∥2dρ(z,y),
(66)

with

k(x,x′)=∫⟨ψ(x,z),ψ(x′,z)⟩ dρ(z,y),g(x)=−∫⟨y,ψ(x,z)⟩ dρ(z,y).k(x,x') = \int\dotp{\psi(x,z)}{\psi(x',z)}\d\rho(z,y), \qquad g(x) = -\int\dotp{y}{\psi(x,z)}\d\rho(z,y).k(x,x′)=∫⟨ψ(x,z),ψ(x′,z)⟩dρ(z,y),g(x)=−∫⟨y,ψ(x,z)⟩dρ(z,y).
(67)

Thus

δf(α)(x)=∫k(x,x′) dα(x′)+g(x),∇ ⁣Wf(α)(x)=∫∇xk(x,x′) dα(x′)+∇xg(x).\delta f(\alpha)(x) = \int k(x,x')\d\alpha(x')+g(x), \qquad \Wgrad f(\alpha)(x) = \int\nabla_x k(x,x')\d\alpha(x')+\nabla_x g(x).δf(α)(x)=∫k(x,x′)dα(x′)+g(x),∇W​f(α)(x)=∫∇x​k(x,x′)dα(x′)+∇x​g(x).
(68)

These kernels are generally not convex in the particle variable, so the geodesic-convex convergence theory above does not apply directly.

show_book_figure("gradflow-mlp-homogeneous-relu")

Mean-field training of a homogeneous two-layer model as transport in neuron space. The left panel shows the Wasserstein particle gradient flow in the reduced homogeneous coordinates (∣u∣v1,∣u∣v2)(|u|v_1,|u|v_2)(∣u∣v1​,∣u∣v2​), with black dashed rays marking the teacher directions. The right panel shows the weighted angular density along a front-loaded sequence of times, colored from red to blue, so that the early concentration of neuron directions is visible. The display follows the rendering of the auxiliary MLP experiment but keeps only the W2W_2W2​ flow, not the spectral-flow comparison.

The interactive demo gives a lightweight version of the same phenomenon: particles move in reduced neuron coordinates, while their angles concentrate around the teacher directions.

Classical Convexity and Stationarity¶

Before using the specific homogeneity mechanism of Chizat and Bach, it is useful to isolate a simpler convex-analytic principle behind many mean-field arguments. Consider an energy

F(α)=12∬k(x,x′) dα(x) dα(x′)+∫V(x) dα(x)+CF(\alpha) = \frac12\iint k(x,x')\d\alpha(x)\d\alpha(x') + \int V(x)\d\alpha(x) +CF(α)=21​∬k(x,x′)dα(x)dα(x′)+∫V(x)dα(x)+C
(69)

on probability measures over a parameter domain. Assume that the quadratic part is convex in the classical affine structure of measures:

Q((1−s)α+sβ)≤(1−s)Q(α)+sQ(β),Q(α)=12∬k dα dα.Q((1-s)\alpha+s\beta) \leq (1-s)Q(\alpha)+sQ(\beta), \qquad Q(\alpha)=\frac12\iint k\d\alpha\d\alpha.Q((1−s)α+sβ)≤(1−s)Q(α)+sQ(β),Q(α)=21​∬kdαdα.
(70)

This is ordinary convexity of the functional on the convex set of measures, not displacement convexity along W2\Wass_2W2​ geodesics.

Proposition: Affine Convexity and Stationary Densities

Let F=Q+∫V dα+CF=Q+\int V\d\alpha+CF=Q+∫Vdα+C be as above, and assume that QQQ is classically convex. Suppose that a Wasserstein gradient flow for FFF converges to a measure α∞=ρ∞ dx\alpha_\infty=\rho_\infty\d xα∞​=ρ∞​dx. Assume the standard regularity needed to pass to the limit in the first variation, and assume that the support and positivity of ρ∞\rho_\inftyρ∞​ allow the stationary condition to be tested against all admissible zero-mass density perturbations. In the form needed here, assume that this stationarity yields, for every competitor β\betaβ, the variational inequality

∫δF(α∞)(x) d(β−α∞)(x)≥0.\int \delta F(\alpha_\infty)(x)\d(\beta-\alpha_\infty)(x)\geq0.∫δF(α∞​)(x)d(β−α∞​)(x)≥0.
(71)

Then α∞\alpha_\inftyα∞​ is a global minimizer of FFF.

Proof Sketch

The dissipation identity for the gradient flow gives stationarity of the limit: formally, after passing to the limit,

∫∥∇δF(α∞)∥2 dα∞=0.\int\norm{\nabla\delta F(\alpha_\infty)}^2\d\alpha_\infty=0.∫∥∇δF(α∞​)∥2dα∞​=0.
(72)

Without support and positivity assumptions, this identity only controls the first variation on the region explored by the limit. The density hypothesis allows one to test against sufficiently many signed density perturbations of total mass zero. By approximation and the assumed regularity, this yields the displayed first-order variational inequality for arbitrary competitors β\betaβ. Classical convexity of FFF in the affine variable α\alphaα then gives the usual subgradient inequality

F(β)≥F(α∞)+∫δF(α∞) d(β−α∞)≥F(α∞).F(\beta) \geq F(\alpha_\infty) + \int\delta F(\alpha_\infty)\d(\beta-\alpha_\infty) \geq F(\alpha_\infty).F(β)≥F(α∞​)+∫δF(α∞​)d(β−α∞​)≥F(α∞​).
(73)

Thus no competitor has smaller energy. For square-loss two-layer mean-field models, (66) is exactly of this quadratic-plus-linear form, and positive semidefiniteness of the induced kernel kkk is the classical convexity assumption.

The mean-field description of two-layer training was developed in several works, including Chizat & Bach, 2018Mei et al., 2018. The distinctive contribution of Chizat and Bach is a global-convergence analysis for positively homogeneous networks without adding an explicit regularizer or relying on noisy SGD to create a Laplacian term. The following formal statement isolates the core mechanism and ignores the technical issues due to ReLU non-smoothness, support propagation and compactness.

Proposition: Formal Global Optimality for Two-Homogeneous Mean-Field Flows

Assume that the feature is positively two-homogeneous in the neuron variable,

ψ(λx,z)=λ2ψ(x,z)(λ>0),\psi(\lambda x,z)=\lambda^2\psi(x,z) \qquad(\lambda>0),ψ(λx,z)=λ2ψ(x,z)(λ>0),
(74)

and that f(α)=J(Gα)f(\alpha)=J(G_\alpha)f(α)=J(Gα​) with JJJ convex and differentiable as a functional of the predictor. Let α\alphaα be a smooth stationary point of the Wasserstein flow, so that ∇xδf(α)(x)=0\nabla_x\delta f(\alpha)(x)=0∇x​δf(α)(x)=0 on supp⁡(α)\operatorname{supp}(\alpha)supp(α). Assume also full directional support: for every nonzero direction ω\omegaω, the support of α\alphaα intersects the ray {λω:λ>0}\{\lambda\omega:\lambda>0\}{λω:λ>0}. Then α\alphaα is a global minimizer of fff over the mean-field model class.

Proof

Write

hα(x)=δf(α)(x)=⟨∇J(Gα),ψ(x,⋅)⟩ρ.h_\alpha(x) = \delta f(\alpha)(x) = \left\langle\nabla J(G_\alpha),\psi(x,\cdot)\right\rangle_\rho.hα​(x)=δf(α)(x)=⟨∇J(Gα​),ψ(x,⋅)⟩ρ​.
(75)

By two-homogeneity of ψ\psiψ, hα(λx)=λ2hα(x)h_\alpha(\lambda x)=\lambda^2h_\alpha(x)hα​(λx)=λ2hα​(x). Normalize a nonzero direction ω\omegaω and choose rω>0r_\omega>0rω​>0 with rωω∈supp⁡(α)r_\omega\omega\in\operatorname{supp}(\alpha)rω​ω∈supp(α). Stationarity gives a zero radial derivative at this point:

0= d drhα(rω)∣r=rω=2rωhα(ω).0 = \frac{\d}{\d r}h_\alpha(r\omega)\bigg|_{r=r_\omega} = 2r_\omega h_\alpha(\omega).0=drd​hα​(rω)∣∣​r=rω​​=2rω​hα​(ω).
(76)

Hence hα(ω)=0h_\alpha(\omega)=0hα​(ω)=0 for every direction ω\omegaω, and by homogeneity hα(x)=0h_\alpha(x)=0hα​(x)=0 for every xxx.

For any competitor β\betaβ, convexity of JJJ gives

f(β)−f(α)≥∫hα(x) d(β−α)(x)=0.f(\beta)-f(\alpha) \geq \int h_\alpha(x)\d(\beta-\alpha)(x)=0.f(β)−f(α)≥∫hα​(x)d(β−α)(x)=0.
(77)

Thus no competitor has smaller risk. The rigorous theorem replaces the full directional support assumption by propagation and overparameterization hypotheses ensuring that a negative descent direction would be present in the support and would contradict stationarity.

References¶
  1. Otto, F. (2001). The geometry of dissipative evolution equations: the porous medium equation. Communications in Partial Differential Equations, 26(1–2), 101–174.
  2. Ambrosio, L., Gigli, N., & Savaré, G. (2006). Gradient Flows in Metric Spaces and in the Space of Probability Measures. Springer.
  3. Benamou, J.-D., Carlier, G., Mérigot, Q., & Oudet, E. (2016). Discretization of functionals involving the Monge–Ampère operator. Numerische Mathematik, 134(3), 611–636.
  4. Peyré, G. (2015). Entropic approximation of Wasserstein gradient flows. SIAM Journal on Imaging Sciences, 8(4), 2323–2351.
  5. Gallouët, T. O., & Monsaingeon, L. (2017). A JKO splitting scheme for Kantorovich–Fisher–Rao gradient flows. SIAM Journal on Mathematical Analysis, 49(2), 1100–1130.
  6. Carrillo, J. A., Chertock, A., & Huang, Y. (2015). A finite-volume method for nonlinear nonlocal equations with a gradient flow structure. Communications in Computational Physics, 17(01), 233–258.
  7. Gianazza, U., Savaré, G., & Toscani, G. (2009). The Wasserstein gradient flow of the Fisher information and the quantum drift-diffusion equation. Archive for Rational Mechanics and Analysis, 194(1), 133–220.
  8. Maas, J. (2011). Gradient flows of the entropy for finite Markov chains. Journal of Functional Analysis, 261(8), 2250–2292.
  9. Erbar, M. (2010). The heat equation on manifolds as a gradient flow in the Wasserstein space. Annales de l’Institut Henri Poincaré, Probabilités et Statistiques, 46(1), 1–23.
  10. McCann, R. J. (1997). A convexity principle for interacting gases. Advances in Mathematics, 128(1), 153–179.
  11. Cordero-Erausquin, D., McCann, R. J., & Schmuckenschläger, M. (2001). A Riemannian interpolation inequality à la Borell, Brascamp and Lieb. Inventiones Mathematicae, 146(2), 219–257.
  12. von Renesse, M.-K., & Sturm, K.-T. (2005). Transport inequalities, gradient estimates, entropy and Ricci curvature. Communications on Pure and Applied Mathematics, 58(7), 923–940.
  13. Villani, C. (2009). Optimal Transport: Old and New (Vol. 338). Springer.
  14. Lott, J., & Villani, C. (2009). Ricci curvature for metric-measure spaces via optimal transport. Annals of Mathematics, 169(3), 903–991.
  15. Sturm, K.-T. (2006). On the geometry of metric measure spaces. I. Acta Mathematica, 196(1), 65–131.
